item.title: It's time for yet another unhinged XL Tuesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
those pancakes look good
they should probably start a company
nothing like some good banter
right now
*stolen
reminds me of a Simpsons episode
ideal
he's got a little N64
mmm hmm
as is tradition
i'm convinced
dogs don't wear suits and glasses!
always
good times
sick technique if you can pull if off well
i knew it
that's my time.. have a great night gang!!
item.dc:creator: CeleryMan7

item.title: One of us shared his personal experience, and asks that we hold our loved ones close.
item.description: @JKBenton you are one of my favorite imgur artists, and loved by many. I'm so glad your son is recovering. I hope by sharing your story people will drive more carefully, and keep an eye out for pedestrian and bike riders; especially at crosswalks! I don't go on insta often, but came across this today.
item.dc:creator: TripUpStairs

item.dc:creator: itoscaresme

item.title: Conquered my Plateau!!
item.description: Hit 273lbs finally! I was stuck at 280lbs for at least a month and a half. I was discouraged, dissatisfied and depressed. Mentally I was checked out. This is when I knew I had to ask for help. I posted to multiple groups and each one someone had given me great advice! Here’s what I did to break it: I took everything everyone told me to do and stuck with it! I counted macros closer than ever, I worked out harder. I took body measurements (neck, bust, arms, calves, thighs, waist, hips, etc) and counted every calorie. Now I’ve got more drive than ever. Most importantly I didn’t quit. Mentally I was fried and done but I knew I had to push through. Again thank you to everyone who helped. This means a lot to me, this is the first time I’ve stuck with weight loss. Here are some before and after pics!
item.dc:creator: PapaBjorno

item.title: True words from the C.L.I.T. Commander himself
item.dc:creator: Sensiblyinteresting

item.title: The life I wanted
item.description:
I made a fun post yesterday bragging about my dog but today's post is more serious It contains themes of addiction, depression, death and recovery.
Some of you know I'm in recovery after a 17 year addiction and 4 years of homelessness with my dog There was one specific point I remember my mental health being the lowest of all those years.
I was homeless in St Paul but my best friend Elisa did everything to take care of me. She was my hope and she did more for my dog than I ever could. She ran her own animal rescue and she had the life I wanted. When Elisa passed away abruptly in 2016 it felt like the final straw for me, the end of the line.
It was everywhere. Every news station, every radio show every fucking text and phone call. Bride found dead on her wedding night and everyone in the world knew it was murder except the police.
As a homeless addict my life wasn't exactly going well anyway, but I always had hope that I would see the other side before she died. She treated me like a human being when many didn't. Her funeral was a few days later, I gave the eulogy and spoke about all the times she bought me groceries and helped me cook when I wasn't eating. I left the funeral and started planning the end of my pathetic life.
A friend of a friend reached out that night after hearing what I was going through, even my hometown 400 miles away heard the news story. I remember sitting in his truck, half gallon of gin deep and just bawling my fucking eyes out.
His wife Lacey and him agreed to take me in, let me and my dog stay at their home while I got back on my feet I had talked to these people all of 10 minutes and they opened their home to me and my dog it was incredible.
They didn't just give me a place to stay They gave me the hope I so desperately needed, and they treated me like a person who just needed love.
They volunteered for a rescue league in St Paul and their house was filled with dogs Being there showed me what life could really be like - comfortable and surrounded by foster dogs - just like Elisa was. They first showed me the life that I worked so hard to have now.
Despite doing everything in their power to help me, I still needed professional care. When that became obvious they helped me get set up at the psych ward and took care of my dog while I was admitted.
Lacey and Tony are just a short time of my story, but they played a bigger role in my recovery than they'll ever know. I eventually got sober and back on my feet 6 years ago. When I moved back to Minneapolis this year I was excited to see which rescue Lacey was volunteering for so I could help.
And this amazing woman started her own rescue that's now saved over 3,000 dogs all over the country!!! So I jumped in and brought home my first Minnesota foster dog last week
If Elisa was alive today I'm pretty sure she'd see my life and say - "See! I told you so Abe" She was always so confident that things were going to get better, and sometimes we all desperately need an Elisa in our lives.
I posted a fundraiser yesterday for Lacey's Healing Hearts Rescue because I believe in everything they do, but I wasn't sure if I could stomach telling everyone my past connection. Imgurians raised $2,500 in 24 hours and started sending dozens of items to help dogs in need all over the country. I've done a lot of these fundraisers but this one is way more personal, a lot harder to talk about, and I can't thank you all enough for being supportive.
Life looks a lot different now than it did 10 years ago, I'm glad I didn't end it before things turned around. I may not have Elisa here to thank for all the love, but if I live a life that would've made her proud - she will always be a part of me. ... If you're able to donate to help rescue dogs in honor of Elisa it would mean so much to me, check out the Healing Hearts website below - https://healingheartsrescue.org/donate/ ... If you'd like to send supplies to Healing Hearts and support them in rescuing dogs all over the country, check out their wishlist here - https://www.amazon.com/hz/wishlist/ls/35V4LNUKMQJVT
item.dc:creator: MrPuckett

item.title: Damn, Dam: Minnesota dam faces 'imminent failure' amid rising flood waters
item.description: Residents living near Minnesota's Rapidan Dam are being warned that the structure is in "imminent failure condition" due to rising flood waters and heavy damage from accumulating debris.

item.dc:creator: PWBoiler

item.title: Things seen this week during structural assessments!
item.description:
Does anyone want the plumber's special? It involves removing all structural support for your home.
A landslide buried a big portion of this house, leaving it with severe damage.
Hillside homes are more prone to landslide damage, making it crucial to prepare for such events. Proper drainage systems, retaining walls, and regular upkeep can help mitigate the risks and protect your home from such disasters.
The constant land movement in Portuguese Bend has compromised the structural stability of this home, leading to uneven settling and huge visible cracks.
Someone attempted a DIY retrofit using cables, turnbuckles, and some bolts. DIY methods are not recommended, especially for something as crucial as the structural support of your home. A better approach would be to use URFP steel plates to securely anchor the foundation to the mudsill.
Here we have a failing retaining wall that is leaning over into the property, posing a significant hazard. It is crucial to address this issue promptly to prevent further damage or potential safety risks.
In another DIY project we came across, someone used a railroad track as a girder to support this home. This unconventional approach is not suitable for properly providing the necessary structural support.
A video is worth a thousand words. Thank you for joining us, we hope you enjoyed! Check out our Instagram for more exciting content, and we'll see you next Tuesday at 2 pm! https://www.instagram.com/alphastructural/
item.dc:creator: AlphaStructural

item.title: A real mixed bag
item.description: What a fucking day - I actually thought I got ghosted by the interviewer but they have Covid and apologized for missing the scheduled time. We finally connected and in spite of me nervous talking like a goon I came away with a job! Now I just have to read the contract and make sure it's not ridiculous. But then things took a turn for the horrible, unrelated to the job thing, when I heard a clattering in the kitchen and discovered that the mouse problem we thought was resolved was not in fact resolved, and a mouse managed to get caught but not killed in one of the traps we still had set just in case. This thing is screaming and trying to chew its own leg off, I can't find our bucket or a pair of gloves anywhere and tbh I've never had to deal with this. They've always died instantly in the trap, which is humane. I hate the poison (we have a pet friendly building plus it fucks with the bird population) and the sticky traps are horrifying so quick kill traps are basically the least worst option, imo. Anyway, my neighbour came home and when I told him what happened he came up and CRUSHED ITS HEAD WITH A PAIR OF PLIERS and now I have to lay down for a bit.
item.dc:creator: ImmortalChild
